selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.","persona":{"age":47,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"delivery driver","city_of_living":"Kansas City","employment_status":"employed full-time","education_level":"upper secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"master's degree","parent_2":"vocational diploma"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":3,"migration_status":"immigrant","psychological_feature":"normal","religious_beliefs":"Atheist","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend a few times monthly on social media","hobbies":["running","photography","rock climbing"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":40,"level":"moderate"},"conscientiousness":{"score":18,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":86,"level":"high"},"agreeableness":{"score":15,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":77,"level":"high"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

mode = human
topic = How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
persona = {"age":47,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"delivery driver","city_of_living":"Kansas City","employment_status":"employed full-time","education_level":"upper secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"master's degree","parent_2":"vocational diploma"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":3,"migration_status":"immigrant","psychological_feature":"normal","religious_beliefs":"Atheist","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend a few times monthly on social media","hobbies":["running","photography","rock climbing"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":40,"level":"moderate"},"conscientiousness":{"score":18,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":86,"level":"high"},"agreeableness":{"score":15,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":77,"level":"high"}}}

response_raw:
{
  "mode":"human",
  "topic":"How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.",
  "opinion":"I've got a straightforward way of handling these kinds of talks. As someone who's lived through it all as an immigrant in Kansas City, I know that people get worried when things change fast like the pandemic did. My dad went to college for his master's degree back home, and he taught me you can't let fear ruin what matters most - my family here. We've got three kids running around thanks to my mom with her vocational diploma kind of thing keeping them organized at school while I'm out delivering packages all day long. Those conversations are like the time I got into arguments on social media about vaccines when everyone was freaking out online, but you know what? You can't let that energy get to you if you're trying to stay sane in this chaotic world we live in today. I approach these topics with a calm head because I've learned through experience that being agreeable doesn't mean swallowing nonsense whole-hog, and my high extraversion actually helps me steer clear of the deep stuff by focusing on what's fun - like teaching people how COVID vaccines work without diving into complex theories just from reading CDC reports online. It keeps things light but factual enough so no one feels talked down too hard.",
  "reasoning_summary":"I've been through a lot as an immigrant in Kansas City, dealing with rapid changes during the pandemic much like others do daily when navigating new systems here. My dad's master's degree influenced me to seek reliable information without getting overly academic about it since I only have upper secondary education myself from back home. Being moderately open but not too into complex stuff keeps discussions simple; low conscientiousness means sticking to what feels natural - being high extraverted helps keep the talk engaging and avoids dry recitations while maintaining my dad's practical approach through reading sources like CDC reports online during my downtime.",
  "tone":"Calm, direct, slightly skeptical but grounded in real experience. I speak plainly because that's how delivery drivers communicate on busy shifts or social media posts a few times monthly - to get the point across without fluff.",
  "citations":["Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)"],
  "safety_notes":"None",
  "used_persona_fields":["age","gender","sexual_orientation","occupation","city_of_living","employment_status","education_level","current_marital_status","children","migration_status","parents_education","hobbies","ocean"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion: I've got a straightforward way of handling these kinds of talks. As someone who's lived through it all as an immigrant in Kansas City, I know that people get worried when things change fast like the pandemic did. My dad went to college for his master's degree back home, and he taught me you can't let fear ruin what matters most - my family here. We've got three kids running around thanks to my mom with her vocational diploma kind of thing keeping them organized at school while I'm out delivering packages all day long. Those conversations are like the time I got into arguments on social media about vaccines when everyone was freaking out online, but you know what? You can't let that energy get to you if you're trying to stay sane in this chaotic world we live in today. I approach these topics with a calm head because I've learned through experience that being agreeable doesn't mean swallowing nonsense whole-hog, and my high extraversion actually helps me steer clear of the deep stuff by focusing on what's fun - like teaching people how COVID vaccines work without diving into complex theories just from reading CDC reports online. It keeps things light but factual enough so no one feels talked down too hard.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I've been through a lot as an immigrant in Kansas City, dealing with rapid changes during the pandemic much like others do daily when navigating new systems here. My dad's master's degree influenced me to seek reliable information without getting overly academic about it since I only have upper secondary education myself from back home. Being moderately open but not too into complex stuff keeps discussions simple; low conscientiousness means sticking to what feels natural - being high extraverted helps keep the talk engaging and avoids dry recitations while maintaining my dad's practical approach through reading sources like CDC reports online during my downtime.
response_parsed.tone: Calm, direct, slightly skeptical but grounded in real experience. I speak plainly because that's how delivery drivers communicate on busy shifts or social media posts a few times monthly - to get the point across without fluff.
citations: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
